content.problemStatement:
You are given an integer NNN and a string SSS, whose length is NNN. Your task is to split the string SSS into kkk consecutive substrings of the same length, so that the splitting is magical, while maximizing the kkk.

We call a splitting magical if all of the substrings are anagrams of each other. We say that two string AAA and BBB are anagrams of each other if we can rearrange the characters of the string AAA so that it becomes equal to string BBB.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of testcases. Then the testcases follow.
Each testcase contains of a single line of input, an integer NNN and a string SSS.


Output Format
For each testcase, output in a single line maximal possible value of kkk. It can be showed that the answer always exists.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 200000
S consists of printable ASCII characters (the solution works for any characters).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains an integer N and a string S of length N, separated by a space.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximal possible value of k, the number of equal‑length consecutive substrings into which S can be split so that all substrings are anagrams of each other.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For "abbaab" the frequencies are a:3, b:3, gcd=3, so we can split into 3 parts. For "aaaa" all 4 a's give gcd=4. For "abcdef" each character appears once, gcd=1.
In the first case each character appears 3 times, gcd=3. In the second case frequencies are a:4, b:2, c:1, d:1, gcd=1.
Each of the six characters appears twice, gcd=2, so the string can be split into 2 magical substrings.

solution.approach: For a splitting into k equal parts to be magical, each character must appear the same number of times in every part. Therefore the total count of each character in the whole string must be divisible by k. Let freq[c] be the total occurrences of character c. The condition is: for all c, freq[c] % k == 0. Hence k must divide every freq[c]; the largest such k is the greatest common divisor (GCD) of all frequencies. The answer for each test case is simply G = gcd of all character frequencies in S. The algorithm: count frequencies, compute their GCD, output it. This runs in O(N) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the length of the string. Overall O(total N) across all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a fixed size array (e.g., 256 integers) is used for frequency counts.
